topic: A person accidentally sends a text to the wrong recipient
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my screen, jarringly out of context. It was a message from 'Marcus,' a name I hadn't seen in months. My heart gave a sudden, unwelcome thump against my ribs. I stared at the screen, the blue light reflecting in my wide eyes. The content of the text was innocuous enough—a simple, "Running late for dinner tonight." But the timing, the sheer unexpectedness of it, sent a ripple of unease through me. I checked my contacts list, my fingers hovering over the keyboard, a strange tension tightening my jaw. Who would be texting me now? I reread the message three times, searching for some hidden meaning, finding nothing but a casual update. A cold knot formed in my stomach as I decided to ignore it, but the silence in the room felt loud, amplifying the lingering question.

[story 2]

I felt a prickle crawl up my neck as my phone buzzed. It was a message, sent from an unknown number. My breath hitched. I picked up the device, my thumb tracing the screen with a deliberate slowness. The message read: "Did you remember the blue folder?" It was completely out of place; I hadn't been organizing anything related to a 'blue folder.' A quick glance at my recent communications revealed nothing matching that sender. I leaned back in my chair, my posture stiffening, my gaze fixed on the phone as if it held some hidden key. My hands started to tremble slightly as I tried to rationalize the intrusion, but the feeling of being watched, of something being deliberately misplaced, kept my muscles coiled tight.

[story 3]

It was a simple reply, yet it threw me off balance. A text arrived, clearly addressed to someone else, but somehow directed at me. "The meeting is postponed until Tuesday," it read. My initial reaction was a sharp intake of breath. I checked my phone again, my movements jerky as I scanned the thread. The context was entirely absent; I hadn't scheduled any meeting that required rescheduling. A low hum of anxiety vibrated beneath my skin. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small space between the sofa and the window. Every shadow seemed to shift, and I kept glancing toward the door, waiting for something to materialize from the periphery.
